content.full_text:
Steven R. Meyers 
City Attorney 
835 E. 14th Street 
TKhnlcal J. .. I"once 
(916) 322 • .5662 
San Leandro, CA 
94577 
Dear Mr. Meyers: 
Administration 
322-5660 
July 23, 1984 
beclltlVltll <t901 
322..5901 
Enforcement 
322-6oUt 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice, 
Our Advice No. A-84-162 
Thank you for your letter requesting advice on the proper 
method for valuing the gift to a public official of a ticket to 
a charitable fundraising event. 
As you noted in your letter, I 
recently advised Judge Charles G. Rubin (Advice Letter No. 
A-84-01S) that the value of such a gift was the value of the 
event itself and not the portion of the ticket price that 
represents a charitable contribution. 
You asked how this 
apportionment between the reportable event value of the ticket 
and the donation value should be made if the ticket does not 
spell it out. 
The value of all non-monetary gifts is based on a good faith 
estimate of their fair market value. 
Thus, the reportable value 
of a ticket to a charitable event should be based on a good 
faith, reasonable estimate of the fair market value of the 
meals, entertainment, etc., provided at the event. 
Precise 
guidelines for making this determination are impossible. 
In 
most cases, it is likely that the sponsor of the event would be 
happy to provide such information especially since many people 
need this type of information for completing their income tax 
returns. 
In addition, it is reasonable to assume that the 
donation value of the ticket exceeds the reportable value. 
I trust that this letter answers your concerns. 
If I can be 
of further assistance, please feel free to contact me at 
~U6/322-5901. 
DMF: km 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
A
I/, 
' 
'---L 
/~' 
w 
~/~.~ 
,

Ie A..-L /~f/'- '~/" ,;;->1" ___ ,_ .. 
Diane Maq;' Fishburn 
Counsel, Legal Division 


City of San Leandrb 
... ' 
Civic Center, 835 E.14th S~' 
j~ 
!oJ' 
oJ 
10 .10 
San Leandro, California 9 
~~ 
Office of City Attorney 415-577-3361 
June 13, 1984 
Diane Maura Fishburn, Staff Counsel 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 "K" St. Building -
Legal Division 
Sacramento, California 95814 
RE: Your File * A-84-015 
(Advice Letter To Charles G. Rubin) 
Dear Ms. Fishburn: 
I recently had occasion to review your Advice Letter 
* A-84-015 to the Honorable Charles G. Rubin. 
I am somewhat 
confused by one statement which you made in that letter. 
You 
indicate that the reportable value of a ticket for a charitable 
event given to an official is the "value of the event itself ... 
the portion of the ticket price that represents a charitable 
contribution is not included in calculating the value of the 
gift." 
In most instances a ticket for a charitable event does not 
separately state the value of the meal or event (the reportable 
portion) and the donation value (the unreportable portion). 
Section 81011 of the Government Code provides that value is 
the " ... estimated fair market value at the time received or expen-
ded." 
Fair market value is the value that the item would command 
in the open market. 
Kaiser Co. vs. Reid 30 Cal. 2d 610, 623 
(1947). 
Thus it would seem to me that if the ticket is sold to 
the general public at a listed price or carries a face value, the 
fair market value is that face value. 
(3 FPPC Opinions 107, 112 
Dec. 8, 1977). 


Diane M. Fishburn 
RE: File t A-84=015 
6/13/84 
Page 2 
If the face value of the ticket is not determinative, what 
guidelines or advice would you offer to enable the recipient to 
make an apportionment. 
Oftentimes that allocation will determine 
whether or not some "gifts" are reported. 
Please understand that 
I have no problem with your conclusion, in fact I endorse it as 
logical and practical, I'm simply unclear as to the advice I 
should give my clients on making an appropriate apportionment. 
To 
put it another way, one's hamburger may be another's filet mignon. 
SRM: dc 
Very, truly yours, 
/ 
/1 
I 
I 
'/ 
)-~ lL··''---___ _ 
. Steven R. Meyirs 
City Attorney

sections.question: You asked how this apportionment between the reportable event value of the ticket and the donation value should be made if the ticket does not spell it out.
sections.conclusion: The value of all non-monetary gifts is based on a good faith estimate of their fair market value. Thus, the reportable value of a ticket to a charitable event should be based on a good faith, reasonable estimate of the fair market value of the meals, entertainment, etc., provided at the event. Precise guidelines for making this determination are impossible. In most cases, it is likely that the sponsor of the event would be happy to provide such information especially since many people need this type of information for completing their income tax returns. In addition, it is reasonable to assume that the donation value of the ticket exceeds the reportable value.
sections.question_synthetic: How should a public official determine the reportable gift value of a charitable event ticket when the ticket price does not separately itemize the event value versus the charitable donation portion?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The reportable value of a charitable event ticket should be determined using a good faith, reasonable estimate of the fair market value of the meals, entertainment, and other benefits provided at the event. Event sponsors can typically provide this valuation information, and it is reasonable to assume the donation portion exceeds the reportable event value.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (City Attorney's letter dated 6/13/84) and FPPC response (7/23/84). Extracted information from the FPPC response portion (A-84-162). Question and conclusion sections clearly identified in response letter. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not significantly impact extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked how this apportionment between the reportable event value of the ticket and the donation value should be made if the ticket does not spell it out.

CONCLUSION: The value of all non-monetary gifts is based on a good faith estimate of their fair market value. Thus, the reportable value of a ticket to a charitable event should be based on a good faith, reasonable estimate of the fair market value of the meals, entertainment, etc., provided at the event. Precise guidelines for making this determination are impossible. In most cases, it is likely that the sponsor of the event would be happy to provide such information especially since many people need this type of information for completing their income tax returns. In addition, it is reasonable to assume that the donation value of the ticket exceeds the reportable value.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel Diane Martz Fishburn responds to City Attorney Steven Meyers' question about how to calculate the reportable gift value when a charitable event ticket does not separately break down event value from donation value. She advises using a good faith estimate of fair market value for the event benefits and suggests contacting the event sponsor for valuation information.
